ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for hotplate beancurd; 'tofu food product' (FOODON_03302757) is the closest parent class covering tofu-based prepared dishes.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers soybean curd / tofu as a food category; no specific code exists for hotplate-prepared tofu dishes in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for hotplate beancurd or sizzling tofu as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical shared Zi Char serving of hotplate beancurd is approximately 300 g, consisting of two to three blocks of firm tofu sizzling in sauce on a cast-iron plate, intended for two to three diners.

nutrient_source.title: Beancurd, firm, fried — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Tofu, fried (NDB 16129) adapted with sauce components
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists fried beancurd (豆腐, firm, fried) at approximately 160–175 kcal/100 g with ~12 g protein, ~11 g fat, ~4 g carbohydrate. Hotplate beancurd adds a savoury sauce (oyster sauce, soy sauce, egg) that increases sodium and slightly raises carbohydrate and fat. Values are adapted from HPB fried beancurd baseline with sauce-component adjustment consistent with USDA SR Legacy fried tofu (NDB 16129: 175 kcal, 11.9 g protein, 11.0 g fat, 5.4 g carbohydrate per 100 g) and HPB data for similar Zi Char tofu dishes. Sodium elevated to reflect sauce contribution.

gi_source.title: Tofu (soybean curd) — Glycemic Index entry; analogous estimate for hotplate beancurd
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database reports tofu / soybean curd at GI ≈ 15 (low). Hotplate beancurd is a prepared tofu dish; the sauce components (oyster sauce, soy sauce) contribute minimal additional glycemic carbohydrate. GI of 15 is used as the closest defensible analogous estimate. GL per serving calculated as: (5.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 300 g serving × 15 GI) / 100 = 2.25, rounded to 2.3.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit hotplate beancurd to small portions due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 480 mg per 100 g (approximately 1440 mg per shared serving), which represents a substantial proportion of the 2000 mg daily sodium target recommended by HPB Singapore.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and moderate carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Hotplate beancurd has a low glycemic index (GI ≈ 15) and a low glycemic load per serving (~2.3), making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-sugar sauces and as part of a balanced meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat; soy protein may be cardioprotective
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (~480 mg/100 g) is a concern for cardiovascular disease management; however, the soy protein in tofu is associated with modest LDL-cholesterol reduction, and the dish's unsaturated fat profile (predominantly polyunsaturated and monounsaturated) is preferable to saturated-fat-heavy alternatives — portion control and sauce reduction are advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beancurd on Hotplate (铁板豆腐, Tiě bǎn dòufu) is a popular Singaporean Zi Char dish in which firm tofu blocks are pan-fried until golden, then placed on a preheated cast-iron hotplate and finished with a savoury sauce made from oyster sauce, dark and light soy sauce, egg, garlic, chilli, and sesame oil. The dish arrives at the table sizzling dramatically, with the sauce caramelising against the hot iron surface. The texture contrasts a lightly crisp exterior with a soft, silken interior, and the flavour is umami-rich, slightly sweet, and mildly spicy. It is a staple of Chinese seafood and Zi Char restaurants in Singapore, commonly ordered as a shared side dish alongside rice. Nutritionally, hotplate beancurd is a moderate-calorie, high-protein, low-GI dish; tofu provides plant-based protein (~9.5 g/100 g), calcium (~120 mg/100 g), and isoflavones. The primary nutritional concern is sodium (~480 mg/100 g) from the sauce, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The low glycemic index (GI ≈ 15) makes it suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. It is dairy-free but contains soy, egg, and gluten (from oyster sauce).
common_user_queries: How many calories in hotplate beancurd?
Is sizzling tofu good for diabetics?
Is hotplate beancurd high in sodium?
Can I eat hotplate tofu if I have high blood pressure?
What sauce is used in hotplate beancurd?
What is Zi Char hotplate tofu?

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo entry exists for 'Beancurd on Hotplate' as a named composite dish. Values are adapted from HPB fried beancurd data and USDA SR Legacy fried tofu (NDB 16129) with sauce-component adjustment. The adaptation is methodologically sound but introduces estimation uncertainty, particularly for sodium and carbohydrate from the sauce. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo fried beancurd and USDA SR Legacy fried tofu (NDB 16129) with sauce-component adjustment; no single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite dish. 2. Sodium is estimated at 480 mg/100 g reflecting typical Zi Char sauce contribution; actual restaurant values may vary from ~350–650 mg/100 g depending on sauce quantity. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB or USDA SR Legacy source entries for fried tofu. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed as negligible/trace in plant-oil-fried tofu per USDA SR Legacy data. 5. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for tofu/soybean curd; no direct GI measurement exists for hotplate beancurd as a composite dish. 6. Halal status is listed as false because oyster sauce and the restaurant context (Marina Seafood House, Riverfront Seafood) are not confirmed halal-certified; consumers should verify with individual restaurants. 7. Cholesterol (~45 mg/100 g) reflects egg contribution in the sauce. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, and other micronutrients are estimated from USDA SR Legacy NDB 16129 and HPB fried beancurd data.
